summary: Julia Evans explores how fear in programming leads to overly conservative decisions and worse software. She distinguishes between healthy caution and paralyzing fear that prevents necessary changes like big refactors or deploys. The post argues that better tools and processes—version control, testing, CI, canary deploys—reduce fear by providing safety nets. Beyond tooling, she emphasizes that blameless postmortems and a culture that doesn't punish mistakes are essential for enabling bold improvements. She also notes that fears can be irrational and contagious, persisting even after objective risks are mitigated.
key_points: Fear creates local maximums—being afraid to make changes prevents dramatic improvements and leads to stagnation around mediocre solutions
Tools like version control, testing, CI, canary deploys, and rollback capability are concrete ways to convert fear into manageable caution
Blameless postmortems are critical because blaming people for mistakes makes them more afraid to make future changes, producing worse software over time
Some fears are irrational and can infect other people, persisting even when objective evidence shows things are usually fine
There is an important distinction between caution (careful, excellent) and fear (which holds you back from doing necessary work)
money_quotes: If you're scared of making changes, you can't make something dramatically better, or do that big code cleanup.
Maybe you can't even deploy the code that you already wrote and tested, because it feels too scary.
You just want to stick what's sort-of-working, even if it's not great.
So if you blame people for breaking things, they'll be more scared to make changes in the future, and you'll end up with worse programs.
Even with good design, w/o tests you will fear change and so the code will rot. With good tests, there's no fear, so you'll clean the code.
I feel like there's this really important line between caution (a++ excellent) and fear (which holds you back from doing necessary work)
Some fears are irrational, and they can infect other people.
key_insight: Fear of breaking things creates local maximums in software quality—investing in safety nets like tests, deploys, and blameless culture converts paralyzing fear into productive caution.
